LO 41C

Head & Neck Anatomy · Block 5

The Nasal Cavity

Boundaries and contents; blood supply and innervation; microarchitecture of the nasal mucosa and conchae

Overview Borders Mucosa Conchae & Meatuses Osteomeatal Complex Blood Supply Innervation Quiz

Overview

GROSS ANATOMY

The nasal cavity (nasal fossa) is a pear-shaped conduit for air passing through the center of the visceral cranium — broader at its base than its apex, with elongated side walls. A midline nasal septum divides it into two chambers. Several coiled bones — conchae — project from the lateral walls. The cavity opens anteriorly as a pair of nares and posteriorly into the nasopharynx via the choanae.

Key Functions
  • Air conditioning — warm, humidify, and filter inhaled air
  • Olfaction — olfactory epithelium at apex
  • Resonance — contributes to voice
  • Mucociliary clearance — traps and clears particulates
Key Subdivisions
  • Vestibule — just behind nares; lined by SSE + vibrissae (hairs)
  • Limen nasi — ridge marking SSE → respiratory epithelium transition
  • Respiratory region — bulk of cavity; PSCCE + rich vasculature
  • Olfactory region — apex; specialized neuroepithelium
Coronal/oblique view of nasal cavity showing crista galli, cribiform plate, frontal sinus, ethmoid sinus, turbinates (conchae), maxillary sinus, and meatuses.
Lateral wall of the nasal cavity showing vestibule, agger nasi, conchae, meatuses, sphenoethmoidal recess, atrium, pharyngeal recess, and auditory tube opening.

Boundaries of the Nasal Cavity

6 WALLS
WallStructures Forming ItNotes
Roof (superior) Nasal bones (ant.) → Cribriform plate of ethmoid (mid.) → Body of sphenoid (post.) CN I (olfactory nerve fibers) perforate the cribriform plate
Floor (inferior) Palatine process of maxilla (ant. ¾) + Horizontal process of palatine bone (post. ¼) = Hard palate; incisive foramen transmits nasopalatine nerve + septal branch of superior labial a.
Medial wall (septum) Perpendicular plate of ethmoid (superoposterior) + Vomer (posteroinferior) + Septal cartilage (anterosuperior) Often deviated; Kiesselbach's area on anteroinferior septum is common nosebleed site
Lateral wall Nasal bone + frontal process of maxilla (ant.) → Ethmoid (mid.) → Perpendicular plate of palatine + medial pterygoid plate (post.) Carries the three conchae (turbinates) projecting medially
Anterior opening Nasal bones + lateral nasal cartilages + alar cartilages Nares (nostrils); vestibule immediately within
Posterior opening (choanae) Vomer (septum) + horizontal processes of palatine + medial pterygoid plates Opens into nasopharynx; auditory tube opens on lateral wall of nasopharynx
Steve's notes: Nasal cavity borders (lateral/medial), conchae & meatuses, features of osteomeatal complex, blood supply overview. Click to enlarge.
Steve's notes: Blood supply of nose (sphenopalatine a., ethmoidal aa., descending palatine a.) and innervation (CN I, palatine nn., sphenopalatine ganglion, nasopalatine n.).

Nasal Mucosa — Microarchitecture

HISTOLOGY
1 · Vestibule
  • Epithelium: Stratified squamous (keratinized → non-keratinized)
  • Special features: Vibrissae (hair follicles), sebaceous glands
  • Location: Immediately within nares
  • Boundary: The limen nasi (a thin ridge) marks the abrupt transition to respiratory epithelium posteriorly
2 · Respiratory Mucosa
  • Epithelium: Pseudostratified ciliated columnar (PSCCE) with goblet cells
  • Lamina propria: Dense, richly vascularized; contains sero-mucous glands
  • Venous sinusoids: Erectile-type vessels in inferior concha allow nasal cycle (alternating congestion)
  • Function: Warms, humidifies, filters air; mucociliary escalator
  • Covers: Most of cavity — lateral wall, septum, floor, conchae
3 · Olfactory Epithelium
  • Epithelium: Specialized pseudostratified; no cilia for mucociliary clearance
  • Cell types: Olfactory receptor neurons, sustentacular (supporting) cells, basal cells
  • Bowman's glands: Sero-mucous glands in lamina propria; secretion dissolves odorants
  • Location: Apex of nasal cavity — roof + upper septum + upper lateral wall
  • Nerve fibers: Unmyelinated CN I axons pierce cribriform plate → olfactory bulb
FeatureVestibuleRespiratory MucosaOlfactory Epithelium
EpitheliumStratified squamousPSCCE + goblet cellsPseudostratified (specialized)
Glands in LPSebaceous glandsSero-mucous glandsBowman's (serous) glands
CiliaVibrissae (hairs)Motile cilia (mucociliary)Non-motile cilia (odorant detection)
VasculatureMinimalRich; venous sinusoids in inferior conchaModerate
FunctionFilter large particlesCondition air; trappingOlfaction
LocationBehind naresBulk of cavityApex (roof + upper septum)
⚡ Kiesselbach's Area (Little's Area)
The anteroinferior nasal septum is the most common site for epistaxis (nosebleed). This area is supplied by five anastomosing arteries: anterior ethmoidal a., posterior ethmoidal a., sphenopalatine a., greater palatine a., and septal branch of superior labial a. The rich anastomotic network here makes it both vulnerable and accessible for cauterization.

Conchae & Meatuses

LATERAL WALL

Three coiled scroll-like bones (turbinates) project medially from the lateral wall, each with a corresponding meatus (air space) beneath it. They dramatically increase mucosal surface area for air conditioning.

ConchaBone of OriginMeatus BeneathWhat Drains Into ItKey Notes
Inferior concha Separate bone (inferior nasal concha) — attached to maxilla Inferior meatus Nasolacrimal duct (via valve of Hasner) Largest concha; richest venous plexus (nasal cycle); NOT part of ethmoid
Middle concha Part of ethmoid bone Middle meatus → semilunar hiatus → infundibulum Frontal sinus (via frontonasal duct), Maxillary sinus (via ostium), Anterior & middle ethmoid air cells Most clinically important meatus; site of osteomeatal complex
Superior concha Part of ethmoid bone Superior meatus Posterior ethmoid air cells Smallest concha; above it = sphenoethmoidal recess
Sphenoethmoidal recess — space above and posterior to superior concha; drains the sphenoid sinus
⚡ Memory: What Drains Where
Inferior meatus = tears (nasolacrimal duct) — why your nose runs when you cry
Middle meatus = F-M-A (Frontal, Maxillary, Anterior ethmoids) — the three major sinuses
Superior meatus = Posterior ethmoids only
Sphenoethmoidal recess = Sphenoid sinus

Osteomeatal Complex (OMC)

CLINICAL
Definition
The osteomeatal complex is a functional drainage unit in the middle meatus, consisting of the opening of the frontal, maxillary, and anterior ethmoid sinuses into a common drainage pathway.
Key Structures of the OMC
  • Ethmoid bulla — an enlarged ethmoid air cell that projects into the middle meatus just above the semilunar hiatus; ostia from frontal, maxillary, and anterior ethmoidal sinuses drain right under or near it
  • Hiatus semilunaris — a semilunar (half-moon) shaped groove in the middle meatus; connects the infundibulum to the middle meatus
  • Infundibulum — a funnel-shaped channel receiving drainage from frontal sinus (frontonasal duct), maxillary sinus (ostium), and anterior ethmoid cells; all drain through the hiatus semilunaris into the middle meatus
  • Uncinate process — a thin bone projection that forms the inferior boundary of the hiatus semilunaris
⚡ Clinical Relevance
Obstruction at the OMC (e.g., mucosal edema, polyps, anatomic variants) blocks drainage of the frontal, maxillary, and anterior ethmoid sinuses simultaneously → chronic rhinosinusitis. The OMC is the primary surgical target in functional endoscopic sinus surgery (FESS).

Blood Supply

VASCULATURE
Arterial Supply
ArterySourceRouteTerritory
Sphenopalatine a. Maxillary a. (3rd part) — terminal branch Through sphenopalatine foramen → lateral wall and septum Lateral wall (lateral nasal branches run along conchae) + posterior septum (posterior nasal septal branches); branch passes through incisive foramen → nasopalatine a.
Descending palatine a. Maxillary a. Pterygopalatine fossa → greater palatine canal → hard palate Anterior septum via ascending branch through incisive foramen (contributes to Kiesselbach's area)
Anterior ethmoidal a. Ophthalmic a. (from ICA) Anterior ethmoidal foramen → anterior cranial fossa → cribriform plate → nasal cavity Anterior-superior lateral wall + anterior septum; major contributor to Kiesselbach's area
Posterior ethmoidal a. Ophthalmic a. (from ICA) Posterior ethmoidal foramen → posterior cranial fossa → cribriform plate Posterior-superior lateral wall + septum
Septal br. of superior labial a. Facial a. → superior labial a. Through upper lip → nasal septum anteriorly Anterior septum (contributes to Kiesselbach's area)
Dual Arterial Supply — Two Systems
Internal Carotid System
Via ophthalmic artery → anterior & posterior ethmoidal arteries
→ supply upper/anterior nasal cavity and septum
External Carotid System
Via maxillary artery → sphenopalatine artery (dominant supply)
→ supply most of lateral wall and septum (posterior)
Kiesselbach's Area (Little's Area)
Anastomotic Network on Anterior Septum
Contributions from 5 vessels anastomose on the anteroinferior septum:
  1. Anterior ethmoidal a. (ICA → ophthalmic)
  2. Posterior ethmoidal a. (ICA → ophthalmic)
  3. Sphenopalatine a. (ECA → maxillary)
  4. Greater palatine a. (ECA → maxillary)
  5. Septal br. of superior labial a. (ECA → facial)

Innervation

SENSORY + SPECIAL + AUTONOMIC
NerveTypeOriginTerritory / Function
Olfactory nerve (CN I) Special sensory Olfactory receptor neurons in olfactory epithelium → olfactory bulb Smell; fibers pierce cribriform plate (vulnerable in basal skull fractures)
Anterior ethmoidal n. General sensory (CN V₁) Nasociliary n. → ophthalmic div. of CN V Anterior-superior lateral wall and septum; tip and ala of nose (external nasal nerve)
Posterior superior nasal nn. General sensory (CN V₂) Pterygopalatine ganglion (maxillary division); posterior nasal branches from palatine nn. Posterior-superior lateral wall and septum; roof of mouth
Nasopalatine nerve General sensory (CN V₂) Pterygopalatine ganglion → crosses septum → incisive foramen Posterior septum; glands on septum; anterior hard palate (behind incisors)
Greater & lesser palatine nn. General sensory (CN V₂) Pterygopalatine ganglion → palatine canal Posterior nasal branches → lateral wall; hard palate (greater) + soft palate (lesser)
Autonomic Innervation
Pterygopalatine (Sphenopalatine) Ganglion — Hub of Nasal Autonomics
Parasympathetic (secretomotor)
Pre-ganglionic: CN VII → greater petrosal nerve → joins deep petrosal n. → vidian (pterygoid) nerve → pterygopalatine ganglion
Post-ganglionic: nasal glands, glands of roof of mouth, lacrimal gland → increased secretion + vasodilation
Sympathetic (vasomotor)
Pre-ganglionic: T1 → superior cervical ganglion
Post-ganglionic: internal carotid plexus → deep petrosal nerve → vidian nerve → pterygopalatine ganglion (pass through without synapsing)
→ vasoconstriction of nasal mucosa
⚡ Vidian Nerve = Greater Petrosal + Deep Petrosal
The vidian (pterygoid) nerve = greater petrosal nerve (PS, CN VII) + deep petrosal nerve (Symp, ICA plexus). It travels through the pterygoid (vidian) canal to the pterygopalatine fossa. Cutting this nerve reduces secretion from nasal glands and the lacrimal gland — used surgically in refractory rhinitis and lacrimation.
Sensory Territory Summary
  • Anterior superior: CN V₁ (anterior ethmoidal n.)
  • Posterior superior: CN V₂ (posterior superior nasal nn. from pterygopalatine ganglion)
  • Septum: CN V₂ — nasopalatine n. (posterior) + anterior ethmoidal n. (anterior)
  • Special sense: CN I (olfaction at roof + upper septum)

🧠 Self-Quiz — LO 41C

1. What three bones form the medial wall (nasal septum) of the nasal cavity?

Perpendicular plate of ethmoid (superoposterior) + Vomer (posteroinferior) + Septal cartilage (anterosuperior).

2. What drains into each meatus? (Inferior / Middle / Superior)

Inferior meatus = nasolacrimal duct.
Middle meatus = Frontal sinus, Maxillary sinus, Anterior & middle ethmoid air cells (via hiatus semilunaris + infundibulum).
Superior meatus = Posterior ethmoid air cells.
Sphenoethmoidal recess (above superior concha) = Sphenoid sinus.

3. What is the limen nasi and what is its histological significance?

The limen nasi is a thin ridge at the posterior border of the vestibule marking the abrupt transition from stratified squamous epithelium (vestibule) to pseudostratified ciliated columnar respiratory epithelium (respiratory region).

4. What is the dominant arterial supply to the nasal cavity, and from which parent artery does it arise?

The sphenopalatine artery — the terminal branch of the maxillary artery (3rd part, external carotid system). It passes through the sphenopalatine foramen and supplies most of the lateral wall and posterior septum.

5. Name the 5 arteries contributing to Kiesselbach's area and their parent vessels.

1. Anterior ethmoidal a. (ophthalmic a. → ICA)
2. Posterior ethmoidal a. (ophthalmic a. → ICA)
3. Sphenopalatine a. (maxillary a. → ECA)
4. Greater palatine a. (maxillary a. → ECA)
5. Septal br. of superior labial a. (facial a. → ECA)

6. Which nerve carries parasympathetic secretomotor fibers to the nasal glands, and what is its composition?

The vidian (pterygoid) nerve = greater petrosal nerve (CN VII preganglionic PS) + deep petrosal nerve (sympathetic from ICA plexus). It travels through the pterygoid canal to the pterygopalatine ganglion, which sends post-ganglionic secretomotor fibers to nasal glands.

7. The inferior concha differs from the middle and superior conchae — how?

The inferior concha is a separate bone (one of the facial bones), attached to the maxilla. The middle and superior conchae are both parts of the ethmoid bone. The inferior concha is also the largest and has the richest venous sinusoid network.

8. What three structures make up the osteomeatal complex (OMC)?

Ethmoid bulla (enlarged ethmoid cell), hiatus semilunaris (semilunar groove), and the infundibulum (funnel channel receiving frontal, maxillary, anterior ethmoid drainage). The uncinate process forms the inferior boundary of the hiatus semilunaris.
